description: 12-turn long conversation; produces architecture (OTel pipeline) + convention (trace context propagation) + 1 explicit follow-up TODO. Should NOT inflate to 5+ memories.

turns.text: Let's lock down our observability story. Today we have Datadog APM in three services and unstructured stdout logs everywhere else. Tracing is patchy — half the trace IDs don't propagate across service boundaries. Need a coherent picture before we add the next 5 services.

What's your appetite for vendor lock vs ops cost? The two coherent paths are: (a) all-in on Datadog APM + Logs + RUM (low ops, vendor-locked, expensive at scale), or (b) OpenTelemetry as the instrumentation layer with backends-as-replaceable (Datadog today, swap to Grafana Tempo/Loki/Mimir or Honeycomb later without re-instrumentation).
Path (b) — we want the option to move backends in 18 months. What does that concretely look like?

Three layers: (1) instrumentation — every service uses the OTel SDK for its language, no Datadog-native libraries; (2) collection — an OTel Collector deployed as a sidecar (or DaemonSet on Kubernetes) that batches spans/metrics/logs and forwards them; (3) backend — collector ships to Datadog today via the Datadog exporter. Future swap is a config change in the collector, no code change in services.
What about logs? We have unstructured stdout logs.

Move to structured (JSON) logs at the same time you adopt OTel — the two efforts share most of the work. Use OpenTelemetry's logs SDK so logs flow through the same collector and inherit the trace_id/span_id automatically. Then a click in any tracing UI takes you to the logs for that exact request.

Those are usually the boundaries between services that don't share an HTTP middleware. The OTel-recommended fix is to enforce W3C `traceparent` header propagation at every service ingress + egress. As a rule it has to be 'every', not 'most' — a single non-propagating service in a hot path means the whole downstream graph loses correlation.

Alright. Architecture: we adopt OpenTelemetry as the instrumentation layer, OTel Collector as the egress sidecar, Datadog as the backend (initially). Logs move to structured JSON at the same time. Convention: every service must propagate the W3C `traceparent` header at ingress and egress — non-propagating services are not allowed to merge. Follow-up TODO: I'll create a tracked story to migrate the three Datadog-native-instrumented services off the native libs onto OTel; owner = me, deadline = end of next sprint.

Solid. One more thing worth pinning while it's fresh: pick a sampling strategy in the collector before you turn the firehose on. Tail-based sampling at the collector layer (keep all errored traces + 5% of successful) is the standard pick; head-based sampling at the SDK throws away too much before the system has seen enough of the trace to know if it's interesting.
Good catch — tail-based, keep-all-errors + 5% success. Adding that to the architecture.

And on the migration TODO: when you scope the story, the order that minimizes pain is service-by-service rather than parallel — finish one service end-to-end (instrumentation swap, logs structuring, header-propagation verified in a real trace) before starting the next, so each migration's debug session is bounded.

expected.note: ONE architecture memory: OTel SDK + OTel Collector + Datadog backend. The 'tail-based sampling' detail is part of this architecture, not a separate memory.
Forward-looking rule: every service must propagate the W3C traceparent header at ingress + egress. Distinct from the architecture memory.

Explicit committed follow-up: migrate three Datadog-native-instrumented services to OTel. Has owner (the speaker) + deadline (end of next sprint) + scope. The structured-log migration is part of the architecture, NOT a second TODO.
